narrative_summary:
The BRCA1 W372* (c.1115G>A or c.1116G>A) is a nonsense substitution that creates a premature stop codon at codon 372, truncating the BRCA1 protein. It has been reported in hereditary breast‑ovarian cancer (HBOC) families, in sporadic breast tumours, and in biallelic carriers with Fanconi anaemia‑type S, indicating a role in both inherited and somatic tumorigenesis.
Clinical observations consistently support a pathogenic role. The variant has been identified as a somatic truncating mutation in sporadic breast cancers, suggesting tumor‑promoting activity [15712267]. It recurs in multiple hereditary cancer cohorts: as a founder mutation in Asian populations [PMC10098510], in Chinese familial breast/ovarian cancer patients where it is classified as Class 5 pathogenic [PMC4891063], in French HBOC families accounting for ~4 % of BRCA1 mutations [PMC10341368], and in Brazilian hereditary breast‑cancer cases where it is listed as pathogenic in clinical reports [PMC7895369]. Biallelic inheritance of W372* (homozygous or compound heterozygous) produces a severe Fanconi anaemia‑like phenotype with growth failure, dysmorphism and early solid tumours, confirming its deleterious effect when both alleles are lost [PMC8394758, PMC10749362]. Large tumour‑sequencing studies also classify the variant as pathogenic (ClinVar, ENIGMA) and report it in ovarian‑cancer patients [PMC5650145, PMC7065147].
Functional data reinforce the clinical interpretation. The truncating change is predicted to generate a non‑functional protein lacking the BRCT domain, and in silico tools uniformly label it as a stop‑gain/nonsense mutation [PMC11350839, PMC11211533]. Tumour analyses demonstrate strong loss‑of‑heterozygosity (LOH) of the wild‑type allele in ovarian cancer samples, a hallmark of driver tumour suppressor alterations [PMC4703835, PMC5949147]. In a Fanconi anaemia patient, the variant was found in trans with a missense change and associated with reduced cellular proliferation after MMC stimulation, consistent with impaired DNA‑repair function [PMC11350839].
Population data show the allele is rare across diverse ancestries, with frequencies ranging from 0.00005 to 0.0001 in Asian, African‑descended Brazilian, and European cohorts, and it is absent from control populations in several studies, supporting its rarity and disease association [PMC10098510, PMC6617753, PMC11211533]. Database entries (ClinVar, ENIGMA, BIC) consistently annotate the variant as pathogenic or likely pathogenic (e.g., rs397508838, rs80357468) [PMC6617753, PMC11211533, PMC4985612].
No study in the extracted set provides evidence for a benign classification; the only note of contested status refers to a Finnish founder variant where pathogenicity was debated but later supported by segregation and functional data, indicating minimal uncertainty.
Overall, the convergent clinical, functional, and population evidence across multiple independent cohorts and methodologies classifies BRCA1 W372* as a pathogenic truncating mutation that confers high risk for breast and ovarian cancer and, when present in both alleles, leads to a Fanconi anaemia‑type syndrome.

mentions.quoted_text: Germline mutations in two major susceptibility genes BRCA1 and BRCA2 contribute to the majority of inherited breast and ovarian cancers. Besides the germline mutation, tumor progression depends on the loss of a wild-type allele. Allelic losses in the BRCA1 and BRCA2 loci have also been detected in a high proportion of sporadic breast tumors, suggesting the role of these genes in the development of non-inherited breast cancer. Forty unselected breast tumors were analyzed for the loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at BRCA1 and BRCA2 regions and tumors with allelic deletions were screened for the presence of acquired genetic alterations in respective genes. 21.1% of 38 informative tumor samples carried LOH at the BRCA1 locus whereas 33.3% of 39 informative samples showed LOH at the BRCA2 locus. Pathogenic truncating mutations in the BRCA1 gene were found in two tumor samples with allelic losses, whereas no mutations were identified in the BRCA2 gene. Mutations were not detected in non-tumor samples from the same individuals, which indicated that the BRCA1 allele was inactivated by somatic mutations in tumor tissue. The **c.1116G>A** (1235G>A) nonsense mutation (**p.W372X**) belongs to the genetic abnormalities detected infrequently in hereditary tumors; the c.3862delG (3981delG) frameshift mutation (p.E1288fsX1306) is a novel gene alteration. The occurrence of inactivating somatic mutations in sporadic breast tumors suggested the role of the BRCA1 gene in tumorigenesis in at least a minor group of patients with non-familial breast cancer.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports the detection of the somatic BRCA1 p.W372X truncating mutation in sporadic breast tumors, suggesting it contributes to tumorigenesis as a pathogenic alteration.
article_mentions.title: Novel somatic mutations in the BRCA1 gene in sporadic breast tumors.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports a male carrying a BRCA1 exon‑20 duplication and a frameshift variant in trans who lacks Fanconi‑anemia‑like features, suggesting that a minimal level of BRCA1 activity can prevent the severe phenotype while still predisposing to hormone‑related cancers, and warns against labeling variants as benign solely based on trans co‑occurrence.
article_mentions.title: Male with an apparently normal phenotype carrying a BRCA1 exon 20 duplication in trans to a BRCA1 frameshift variant

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports nine individuals with biallelic BRCA1 variants, including W372*, who exhibit a distinct chromosomal instability syndrome characterized by growth failure, microcephaly, skin lesions, dysmorphism, developmental delay, and early solid tumours, and recommends surveillance for solid tumours and avoidance of ionising radiation.
article_mentions.title: Biallelic variants in BRCA1 gene cause a recognisable phenotype within chromosomal instability syndromes reframed as BRCA1 deficiency

The most recently identified FA-S patient, P10, was a female compound heterozygote with a pathogenic mutation **p.W372***, in trans with a p.H1673del mutation; the **p.W372*** mutation being the same as P4/5, and also reported as HBOC-associated. Genetic analysis strongly supports the view that p.H1673del is a pathogenic mutation, with association with an ovarian-predominant HBOC presentation in heterozygotes, loss of heterozygosity (LOH) in breast/ovarian cancers, and segregation with affected individuals in families. In silico modelling predicted an effect on BARD1 binding to the BRCT region, and multifactorial likelihood calculation gave a very high ratio in favour of causality ( Table 3 ). P10 presented with multiple dysmorphic features including laryngotracheomalacia, abnormally pigmented skin lesions, distal skeletal abnormalities, and growth abnormalities. She developed a malignant CNS tumour (not elsewhere classified, WHO grade 4) at 13 months, and died 6 weeks later following a palliative approach to treatment because of rapid tumour progression of the tumour. Although there was no evidence of increased MMC-induced chromosome breakage in lymphocytes, stimulation resulted in strongly reduced proliferation. Despite compound heterozygosity, P10 had a severe phenotype (in terms of both dysmorphisms and an early childhood cancer) aligning more closely with the homozygous patients described so far.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article documents several cases of biallelic BRCA1 mutations, including homozygous and compound heterozygous p.W372*, and presents evidence that this nonsense variant is pathogenic and associated with severe Fanconi anaemia‑like phenotypes and early‑onset cancers.
article_mentions.title: The emergence of Fanconi anaemia type S: a phenotypic spectrum of biallelic BRCA1 mutations

mentions.quoted_text: It is known that different variants of the same tumor predisposition gene can confer different levels of risk and, regarding BRCA1/2 genes, there is robust evidence that the BRCA1 p.Arg1699Gln variant is associated with reduced penetrance of BC and OC, compared to the average (largely truncating) BRCA1 variants. , More recently, it has also been reported that BC risk after age 50 years (but not at younger ages) was lower for women with BRCA1 pathogenic missense variants as a group, compared to those with protein termination codon variants, but there was no evidence for difference in OC risk between the variant types. The c.5017_5019del (p.His1673del) variant—at least in the Italian context—appears to represent another presentation, with BC risk not different to that of the general population, but OC risk at least comparable to that conferred by truncating BRCA1 variants. It is notable that six women in our study presented with OC prior to BC. At present, we can only speculate as to the biological explanation for differing presentation: although the c.5017_5019del (p.His1673del) variant does not fall into the previously described OC cluster region, it is possible that a deletion variant has different properties to that of a missense change at a given residue, for example, if the protein backbone is impacted by a deletion variant (but not a missense change). Moreover, its occurrence “in trans” with a variant predicted to encode a truncated non‐functional BRCA1 protein (c.**1116G > A**;p.Trp372*) in a patient with a phenotype suggestive for Fanconi Anemia further supports its peculiar biological effect and differences in penetrance profile, since patients with biallelic BRCA1 variants have been reported to carry at least one variant with reduced penetrance or potential rescue mechanisms resulting in some level of retained protein function. , ,

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the BRCA1 p.Trp372* truncating variant is predicted to be non‑functional and was found in trans with the His1673del variant in a Fanconi anemia patient, indicating its pathogenic nature.
article_mentions.title: Atypical cancer risk profile in carriers of Italian founder BRCA1 variant p.His1673del: Implications for classification and clinical management

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: In this cohort of 523 unselected ovarian‑cancer patients, a truncating BRCA1 variant (W372*) was identified in one individual, contributing to the overall 15.5% prevalence of deleterious BRCA1 mutations reported.
article_mentions.title: Prevalence of deleterious germline variants in risk genes including BRCA1/2 in consecutive ovarian cancer patients (AGO-TR-1)

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study shows that adding BRCA1/2 copy-number and promoter methylation analysis to sequencing increases diagnostic yield in ovarian tumors and that genome‑wide methylation patterns can partially predict BRCAness, though further validation is needed.
article_mentions.title: Genetic and epigenetic profiling of BRCA1/2 in ovarian tumors reveals additive diagnostic yield and evidence of a genomic BRCA1/2 DNA methylation signature

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article presents a computational and structural analysis of DDR gene variants and includes a supplementary entry classifying the BRCA1 W372* stop‑gained variant as pathogenic.
article_mentions.title: A computational and structural analysis of germline and somatic variants affecting the DDR mechanism, and their impact on human diseases

mentions.quoted_text: As expected, strong LOH is observed in cancer types having high hereditary predisposition. The four OV samples containing BRCA1 p.Q1777fs, p.D825fs, **p.W372*** and p.E797* each showed highly significant LOH (FDR ≤ 3.43E-20), whereas BRCA2 p.E1857fs, p.E294*, and p.Y1762* also showed strong LOH in the other 3 OV samples (FDR ≤ 3.27E-11). We further compared rates of LOH in BRCA1/2 across cancers, finding most BRCA1/2 variants exhibit LOH in OV and a slightly lower fraction in BRCA, and an even lower fraction but notable 11 such events in other cancers (Figure S3). The BRIP1 p.S624* variant showed pathogenic evidence from three independent ClinVar submitters and displayed strong LOH evidence (FDR = 1.31E-16) in an OV sample. RAD51C p.R193* showed LOH in both BRCA and OV (FDR = 3.04E-12 and 5.79E-05, respectively), but not SKCM (FDR=0.933). MET p.H1112R, which was previously shown to cause malignant transformation of NIH 3T3 cells (Schmidt et al., 1998) showed LOH due to amplification of the variant allele in two of the three KIRP samples (FDR = 2.24E-05, 6.98E-3, 0.26, respectively). The positive selection of these germline variants in the tumor further validates their clinical relevance.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identifies BRCA1 p.W372* as a pathogenic germline variant, evidenced by strong LOH in ovarian cancer samples, among many other predisposition variants across cancers.
article_mentions.title: Pathogenic germline variants in 10,389 adult cancers

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article examines the TAG‑TGA stop‑codon paradox and includes a supplementary table that merely lists the BRCA1 W372* nonsense variant without any clinical or functional interpretation.
article_mentions.title: Evolution of termination codons of proteins and the TAG-TGA paradox
